item.title: Fiji falls short 14-12 after brillaint start in Cup QF
item.description: The Fiji Airways Men’s National 7s team got off to a strong start, with Vuiviawa Naduvalo scoring a brilliant opening try after slicing through the South African defense to give Fiji a 5-0 lead. Kavekini Tanivanuakula added Fiji’s second try, extending the advantage to 12–0.
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji Airways Men’s National 7s team got off to a strong start, with Vuiviawa Naduvalo scoring a brilliant opening try after slicing through the South African defense to give Fiji a 5-0 lead.

Kavekini Tanivanuakula added Fiji’s second try, extending the advantage to 12–0.

Fiji’s disciplined defense kept the Blitzboks at bay despite several attacking opportunities from the South Africans.

However, Naduvalo was shown a yellow card late in the half, and South Africa capitalized on the numerical advantage.

A well-executed cross-field kick led to their first try, reducing the deficit before halftime. 12-7.

The Blitzboks scored the opening try of the second half to take a 14–12 lead.

The match was fiercely contested, with both sides showing plenty of physicality and determination as they battled for a place in the semifinals.

South Africa was awarded a late penalty and managed the closing stages effectively, running down the clock to secure a hard-fought 14–12 victory and book their spot in the semifinals.

The cup semi final will be played later tonight.

In other results Australia defeated France 26-0, New Zealand thrashed Great Britan 24-5 and Spain defeated USA 17-5.

item.title: Light Up Jittu Gospel Concert to unite and inspire community
item.description: Navitalai Naivalurua · 06/06/2026 People can look forward to great music from local artists and groups such as Ratu, Gibson, Saiva, King Easy, One Crew and Grace at the Light Up Jittu Gospel Concert, which will be held at the Lagilagi Housing volleyball court this afternoon.
item.content:encoded:
Navitalai Naivalurua · 06/06/2026

People can look forward to great music from local artists and groups such as Ratu, Gibson, Saiva, King Easy, One Crew and Grace at the Light Up Jittu Gospel Concert, which will be held at the Lagilagi Housing volleyball court this afternoon.

Inspire Pacific Founder Ben Morrison says the concert was organised not only to inspire young people but also to provide local musicians with a platform to showcase their talents.

He says the organisation has been doing valuable work with youths in the Jittu settlement, and the concert is another way of bringing the community together.

Morrison hopes the event will encourage young people to make positive choices and become better individuals.

He adds that while Jittu settlement may be known for crime, he believes that everyone is created in the image of God.

Morrison says activities for children will begin in the morning, while the gospel concert will run from 4pm to 9pm.

item.title: Backyard farming can help ease cost of living pressures - market vendor
item.description: As food prices continue to rise, a Nausori market vendor is encouraging families to start backyard gardens, saying even a small supply of home-grown vegetables can help ease the rising cost of living. 52-year-old Sashi Prakash of Navuso, Nausori has spent more than a decade selling vegetables at the Nausori Market and has witnessed firsthand the growing financial pressure faced by both vendors and
item.content:encoded:
As food prices continue to rise, a Nausori market vendor is encouraging families to start backyard gardens, saying even a small supply of home-grown vegetables can help ease the rising cost of living.

52-year-old Sashi Prakash of Navuso, Nausori has spent more than a decade selling vegetables at the Nausori Market and has witnessed firsthand the growing financial pressure faced by both vendors and customers.

Prakash , who sells eggplant, beans, okra, jackfruit, chillies, ginger and watermelon, says the increasing cost of vegetables is making it difficult for vendors to earn a profit while keeping produce affordable for families.

She says a bag of eggplant can cost vendors as much as $100, forcing them to carefully balance prices.

She says if they sell a heap for $5, some families cannot afford it, and if they lower the price, they do not make a profit.

Prakash says many customers who visit the market are already struggling with household expenses, and vendors often find themselves caught between rising costs and the realities faced by shoppers.

Apart from rising prices, she says vendors inside the market also face competition from people selling vegetables outside the market.

Prakash says her family also does some farming at home, however, poor drainage in their area remains a major challenge.

HINDI INSERT: Prakash on drainage challenges 4th Ju ne

Prakash believes more families could benefit from planting vegetables at home.

She says while many people have busy work schedules and limited time, even a small backyard garden can help reduce food costs and provide fresh produce for the family.

She hopes more people will consider growing their own vegetables as a practical way to cope with the increasing cost of living and ensure their families have access to healthy food.

item.title: Return & Earn Centre opens at Laqere Market
1,200 Fijians stand in solidarity for environment at PRF roadside campaign
item.description: About 1,200 Fijians stood in solidarity for environmental advocacy as the Pacific Recycling Foundation (PRF) hosted its Fourth Annual Roadside Campaign under the theme Stand in Solidarity to Protect Our Environment in Nadi. The campaign was held to mark World Environment Day and World Oceans Day, also celebrated the Ministry of Environment's decision to reject the Environmental Impact Assessment (
item.content:encoded:
About 1,200 Fijians stood in solidarity for environmental advocacy as the Pacific Recycling Foundation (PRF) hosted its Fourth Annual Roadside Campaign under the theme Stand in Solidarity to Protect Our Environment in Nadi.

The campaign was held to mark World Environment Day and World Oceans Day, also celebrated the Ministry of Environment's decision to reject the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) report for the proposed Waste-to-Energy project in Vuda, with participants highlighting the importance of environmental protection, ocean conservation and responsible waste management.

PRF says participants from 21 schools, organisations, community groups and government agencies took part in the one-hour demonstration, standing shoulder-to-shoulder to raise awareness about environmental protection and the shared responsibility of safeguarding Fiji's ecosystems.

D uring the campaign, participants became the voice of the environment and oceans through placards, messages, performances and advocacy, highlighting environmental issues that often go unheard.

Organisers say the Ministry's decision to reject the Environmental Impact Assessment reflects the importance of evidence-based decision-making, public participation and sustained environmental advocacy in protecting Fiji's future.

The event was followed by the Pacific Recycling Foundation's signature "Safe Space" session, where participants shared their environmental concerns, hopes and aspirations through poetry, speeches, songs, artwork, dance and other creative performances.

Pacific Recycling Foundation Founder Amitesh Deo says the campaign has evolved beyond an event and is increasingly becoming a movement driven by community ownership and collective action.

Deo says when the campaign started four years ago, the aim was to create a space where people could stand together and speak on behalf of the environment and oceans that could not speak for themselves.

He says seeing people from different walks of life unite for a common cause was a reminder that environmental advocacy belongs to everyone.

Deo says the long-term vision is for communities throughout Fiji to organise their own roadside campaigns and stand in solidarity for the environment.

He says the dream is for the initiative to become a national movement, where people dedicate one hour of their time to environmental protection.

Deo adds that growing participation over the years demonstrates increasing environmental awareness and a willingness among communities to become part of the solution.

He says what gives the Foundation hope is not only the number of participants but also the diversity of support , with students, businesses, government agencies, recyclers, community leaders, families and advocates all standing together to send a strong message that environmental protection is a shared responsibility.

The Fourth Annual Roadside Campaign builds on the momentum of previous years and reflects the continued growth of a movement aimed at inspiring environmental awareness, community action and long-term behavioural change.

The Pacific Recycling Foundation thanked all participating schools, organisations, volunteers, supporters and partners who contributed to the success of the campaign.

PRF gave special acknowledgement to Nadi Town Council, Fiji Airways, Vuda Marina, Hot Bread Kitchen, Punjas, Autoworld, Motor Winders, Axial Engineering, Flour Mills of Fiji, Fiji Chemicals Limited, Creative Designs Limited, Professional Security Services Limited, The Kidney Hub Foundation, Pacific Specialist Healthcare and Tanoa Hotels for their support.

Organisers say the support of partners continues to strengthen a growing movement as Fiji works towards a more sustainable future.

item.title: Ricardo sets sights on $1,000 FM96 Tekken Down with Vodafone prize after qualifying
item.description: I plan to win the $1,000 up for grabs at the Fiji Showcase for the Fiji Showcase FM96 Tekken Down with Vodafone.Those were the wor

item.title: Residents turn out in numbers for Muanikau Backyard Bazaar
item.description: People turned out in numbers at the Muanikau Backyard Bazaar held at Veiuto Primary School, where community members showcased and sold fresh produce, homemade goods, baked treats, plants, crafts and second-hand items. Muanikau Community Watch Group Chairman William Parkinson says the Backyard Bazaar was not about fundraising, but about bringing the community together in a relaxed, neighbourly sett
item.content:encoded:
People turned out in numbers at the Muanikau Backyard Bazaar held at Veiuto Primary School, where community members showcased and sold fresh produce, homemade goods, baked treats, plants, crafts and second-hand items.

Muanikau Community Watch Group Chairman William Parkinson says the Backyard Bazaar was not about fundraising, but about bringing the community together in a relaxed, neighbourly setting.

He says the idea was to allow residents of Muanikau and nearby areas to sell produce from their backyards, homemade kitchen goods, and second-hand items, while also creating an opportunity for neighbours to connect.

Parkinson says turnout had been steady since the morning, noting that the seaside venue at Veiuto Primary School provided a vibrant setting with strong community support and a wide variety of stalls.

Insert: Parkinson on organizations 6th June

Sai Prema Foundation and Diabetes Fiji Inc are also conducting health checks including blood sugar tests, blood pressure screening, height and weight measurements, and BMI assessments.

item.title: Hungarian national facing meth charges remains in custody
item.description: A Hungarian national charged with the unlawful importation and possession of methamphetamine has been further remanded in custody. Fonta Laszlo appeared in the Lautoka High Court earlier this week facing one count of unlawful importation of illicit drugs and one count of unlawful possession of illicit drugs.
item.content:encoded:
A Hungarian national charged with the unlawful importation and possession of methamphetamine has been further remanded in custody.

Fonta Laszlo appeared in the Lautoka High Court earlier this week facing one count of unlawful importation of illicit drugs and one count of unlawful possession of illicit drugs.

It is alleged that on 17th April this year, Laszlo unlawfully imported and possessed 4,869.7 grams of methamphetamine.

He was intercepted by authorities at Nadi International Airport, where a search of his baggage allegedly revealed drugs concealed in his luggage.

During the hearing, the State informed the court that disclosures had been filed and served but sought additional time to file the Information.

Defence Counsel raised concerns about the Laszlo's medical condition, saying he would be responsible for the costs of his surgery.

The court was also told that Defence Counsel had been unable to obtain instructions from Laszlo while he was in remand because their interpreter was not allowed entry into the remand facility.

The court advised that arrangements would be made for a suitable interpreter to assist the accused and indicated that Defence Counsel could use the court premises to obtain instructions from him.

The matter has been adjourned to 16th June.

Click here for stories on the Drugs Situation in Fiji

item.description: Permanent Secretary for Environment and Climate Change, Doctor Sivendra Michael appeared on a special fijivillage Straight Talk Wi
